title: Shell Commands ({key::!})
subtitle: Bang sends to the shell. Use as a filter or a one-shot.
summary: :!cmd runs cmd in a shell. :r !cmd inserts its output. :%!cmd filters the buffer through cmd. !{motion}cmd does the same in Normal mode.

blocks.text: Vim doesn't try to do everything itself — it gives you the shell as a tool you can pipe through. Three patterns: run, insert output, filter buffer.

rows: Run current file (% expands to filename)
Normal-mode: filter to next paragraph through sort

blocks.title: The Three Modes of Bang
blocks.text:
**`:!cmd`** — runs `cmd` in `'shell'`, prints output to the message area. Doesn't touch the buffer. Use for one-shot tasks (compile, run tests).

**`:r !cmd`** — runs `cmd`, *inserts* its stdout into the buffer below the cursor.

**`:[range]!cmd`** — sends the range's lines as **stdin** to `cmd`, replaces them with the **stdout**. This is *filter mode*. The classic example: `:%!sort` to sort the file in place; `:%!jq .` to pretty-print JSON.

**Normal-mode `!{motion}cmd`** — same as filter mode, but the range comes from a motion: `!}sort` filters from cursor to next paragraph break. `!ipsort` filters the inner paragraph.

Special: `%` in the command expands to the current filename, `#` to the alternate filename. `:!python %` runs the current Python file.
{key:!}{key:!}{key:s}{key:o}{key:r}{key:t}{key:Enter} sorts the current line through `sort` (the doubled bang means "current line").
